ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean seafood dry noodle (lao mian). FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Singaporean mixed seafood dry noodle with sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean seafood dry noodle dish.

main_ingredients: mixed seafood (prawns, squid, fish cake, clams)
signature sauce (oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, representing a single plate of dry noodles with mixed seafood toppings as typically served at a Singaporean hawker stall or casual restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Mee (Noodles) with Seafood, Dry — Singapore HPB Foodo / USDA SR Legacy composite estimate for mixed seafood noodle dish
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Signature Sauce Mix Seafood Dry Noodle' from Hai Kah Lang. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for 'Fried Hokkien Mee' and 'Seafood Noodle Soup' combined with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked egg noodles, mixed seafood (prawns, squid, fish cake), and typical Singaporean sauce components (oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil). The composite estimate reflects a dry-tossed noodle dish with approximately 50% noodles, 30% mixed seafood, 10% sauce, and 10% vegetables per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg noodles, cooked (GI 52, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean signature sauce mixed seafood dry noodle. GI of 52 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked egg noodles (GI 52, glucose reference, Atkinson et al. 2021). This is the dominant carbohydrate vehicle in the dish. The sauce and seafood components contribute negligible additional glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 70.0) / 100 = 36.4, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving ≈ 70 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and fish-based ingredients
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 520 mg sodium per 100 g (≈2080 mg per 400 g serving), this dish provides over 90% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce portions or limit consumption frequency.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from egg noodles with partial mitigation from protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a glycemic index of approximately 52 (low-medium boundary) but a high glycemic load per serving (~36) due to the large noodle portion; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion, substitute with lower-GI noodles where available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from mixed seafood and egg noodles
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (≈2080 mg per serving) poses a significant cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; saturated fat is moderate at 1.1 g per 100 g, and the mixed seafood provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but overall sodium reduction and portion control are recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Signature Sauce Mix Seafood Dry Noodle (招牌混合海鲜捞面, Zhao Pai Hun He Hai Xian Lao Mian) is a Singaporean Chinese noodle dish featuring springy egg noodles tossed in a rich, savoury house-blend sauce typically composed of oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, sesame oil, and chilli, topped with a generous assortment of mixed seafood including prawns, squid, fish cake, and clams. Unlike soup-based noodle dishes, the dry preparation method involves blanching the noodles and seafood separately before tossing them together with the sauce, resulting in a glossy, flavour-coated dish with a firm, chewy noodle texture and tender seafood. Bean sprouts and spring onions are commonly added for freshness and crunch. The dish is a staple of Singaporean seafood-focused Chinese restaurants and hawker-style eateries. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 9.2 g from the mixed seafood, moderate carbohydrate at 17.5 g from the egg noodles, and relatively low fat at 4.8 g. Sodium content is high at approximately 520 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low-medium boundary) based on the egg noodle base, with a high glycemic load per standard serving due to the large noodle portion, which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The seafood component contributes vitamin B12, zinc, and phosphorus, supporting neurological and immune function.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for comparable Singaporean seafood noodle dishes and USDA SR Legacy data for component ingredients. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked egg noodles (GI 52), which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and restaurant-specific sauce formulation variability.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from HPB Foodo (Singapore) seafood noodle entries and USDA SR Legacy ingredient data; exact values will vary by restaurant preparation and sauce quantity. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish category. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the typical ingredient profile of this dish. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked egg noodles; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 7. Halal status is listed as false because the dish may contain pork-derived ingredients in the sauce at some restaurants; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific restaurant. 8. Price data from GrabFood Singapore (SGD 30.32) reflects a premium restaurant serving and is preserved from the input record for reference only; it is not used in nutritional calculations. 9. The dish is served at Hai Kah Lang, Funan Mall, Singapore, as recorded in the source GrabFood data.
